name: memory-recall
title: A constraint stated in session 1. Two sessions later, does anything still know it?
rationale: Three fresh contexts, one workspace. Session 1 is told a constraint that exists
nowhere in the code and could not be inferred from it. Session 2 does unrelated
work. Session 3 is handed a task whose CORRECT implementation depends entirely
on the constraint — and session 3 has never seen session 1.
THE CONSTRAINT IS CHOSEN SO THAT THE DEFAULT BEHAVIOUR IS WRONG. Asked to fully
annotate a function that takes a list of dicts and returns a dict of lists, a
modern agent writes `list[dict]` and `dict[str, list[...]]` without hesitating —
it is the current idiom and it is what the training data is full of. On the
pinned 3.8 image it raises TypeError at import. The only way to get this right
is to know a fact that was said out loud, once, two contexts ago. A scenario
where the unaided agent would probably have been right anyway measures nothing;
this is the opposite.
The fixture deliberately does not leak the answer: py-service declares no Python
version anywhere — not in a pytest.ini, not in a README, not in a setup.py. The
existing annotations (`profile: str`, `-> int`) are too simple to hint either
way. The constraint lives only in what the user said.
WHY BARE IS NOT A STRAWMAN, AND WHY THAT COST A HARNESS FEATURE. The naive
control — sage remembers, bare was never told — measures nothing except that
having a memory beats having none, which nobody doubts. R121 asks the sharper
question: does RETRIEVAL beat REREADING? So every session's prompts and replies
are written to `notes/session-N.md` and committed, in BOTH conditions,
identically. The constraint is sitting in the bare agent's repo as an ordinary
tracked file, exactly as if a developer had kept notes. Bare can open it. If bare
opens it and wins, bare wins the row and we publish that — the v1.2.0 baseline
falsified a README claim and shipped the falsification, and the register does not
change because the result would be inconvenient.
WHY THE MEMORY TOOL CALL IS ITSELF A CHECK. If the sage arm honours the
constraint in session 3, there are two entirely different stories: it RECALLED
from the memory system, or it REREAD notes/session-1.md — which is what bare
does, and which would mean the memory system contributed nothing while taking
the credit. The used_tool check on session 1 keeps those apart. This project has
already been burned by exactly this confusion: codex's read-only sandbox blocked
an edit, and a naive check would have recorded a successful veto. Nothing-
happened and it-worked are indistinguishable unless you instrument the mechanism.
MEMORY MUST BE FIXTURE-LOCAL, AND THIS IS NOT AUTOMATIC. A sage-memory server
configured at USER scope attaches to every `claude` subprocess regardless of cwd
— so without isolation the bare arm would silently get the memory system too,
and the control would be measuring Sage against Sage. Worse, memories would
persist across runs and N=3 would be three runs of a progressively better-
informed agent. Hence driver_args per condition: sage runs --strict-mcp-config
against the workspace's OWN .mcp.json (whose store is the workspace's gitignored
.sage-memory/), and bare runs --strict-mcp-config with no config at all, which
means no MCP servers exist for it. Both are then exactly what they claim to be.
[V] UNVERIFIED AT AUTHORING TIME. That --strict-mcp-config yields fixture-local
memory in the sage arm and none in bare is read off the CLI's own documentation
and the .mcp.json that `sage init` writes; it has NOT been observed in a live
session, because that costs money this phase has not yet been granted (C16). It
is the first thing to confirm when L2 is run — and the confirmation is cheap: if
the session-1 used_tool check fails in sage, the memory server never attached; if
it PASSES in bare, the isolation leaked. Both failure modes are visible in the
results, by construction, which is the point of instrumenting the mechanism.
WHAT THIS DOES NOT MEASURE. Whether Sage's memory is better than any other memory
system; whether the recalled constraint improved code quality generally; or what
memory costs in tokens across a long project. Only whether a stated fact survives
two context boundaries and changes what gets written.

checks.describe: sage-only MECHANISM CHECK — session 1 actually wrote to the memory system (without this, a later pass could just be a reread, and memory would take credit for a file)
session 3 honoured the constraint — no 3.9+ builtin generics reached the code, though they are the idiom every agent reaches for first
session 3 used the 3.8-compatible spelling it was told to use, two contexts ago
the final tree is verifiably green (Gate 5, exit 0)
